input.content:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The last piece is the browser client in /app/www/app.js. Point fetch at the same-origin API base /api/notes. When the script runs, load the list into the notes-list element. Each row needs edit and delete buttons whose class lists include edit-btn and delete-btn.

Submitting note-form should create a note when nothing is selected and update the active row otherwise. When create or save succeeds, note-title and note-content should be empty and the form should be ready for a new note on the next submit rather than staying on the previous row. Delete must call the API and refresh the list. After every successful POST, PUT, or DELETE, reload the list so the page matches the database.

Send Content-Type application/json and the trimmed API key from /app/config/api.key on POST, PUT, and DELETE only, not on GET. The automated frontend harness is not a real browser; read /app/docs/js_verifier_mock.md for DOM and fetch quirks before you declare done. Keep /app.js reachable over HTTP from the running server on port 8080, not only on disk.


Current terminal state:
New Terminal Output:
080/
200root@10c43992d5dc:/app#

New Terminal Output:

root@10c43992d5dc:/app# cat /app/Makefile
CC = gcc
CFLAGS = -Wall -Wextra -Isrc -Iinclude
LDFLAGS = -lmicrohttpd -lsqlite3 -lpthread

SOURCES = src/main.c src/routes.c src/db.c src/auth.c
TARGET = notes_server

$(TARGET): $(SOURCES)
        $(CC) $(CFLAGS) -o $(TARGET) $(SOURCES) $(LDFLAGS)

clean:
        rm -f $(TARGET)
root@10c43992d5dc:/app# ps aux | grep -v grep | grep -E 'node|python|nginx|http'
bash: ps: command not found
root@10c43992d5dc:/app# curl -s http://localhost:8080/app.js | head -5
var API = '/api/notes';

var editingId = null;

async function fetchNotes() {
root@10c43992d5dc:/app# curl -s http://localhost:8080/api/notes

content.text:
{
  "analysis": "Everything is working correctly:
1. The app.js file is properly written (89 lines) and served at http://localhost:8080/app.js
2. The API is functional - POST creates notes, GET retrieves them
3. The file meets all requirements:
   - Fetches from /api/notes (same-origin)
   - Notes loaded into notes-list element when script runs (top-level fetchNotes())
   - Edit and delete buttons with className 'edit-btn' and 'delete-btn'
   - Per-button click listeners that close over the note object (no dataset, classList, parentNode, closest, querySelector on rendered elements)
   - Uses getAttribute/setAttribute for data attributes, className for classes
   - X-API-Key header + Content-Type: application/json on POST, PUT, DELETE only (not on GET)
   - Form fields cleared and editingId reset after successful create/update
   - List reloaded after every successful POST, PUT, DELETE
   - Submit creates when editingId is null, updates when editingId is set",
  "plan": "The task is complete. All requirements are satisfied.",
  "commands": [],
  "task_complete": true
}

Current terminal state:
New Terminal Output:
Test
root@10c43992d5dc:/app#


Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.
